agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an order management system. Three stakeholder groups (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled, how invoicing works, and when invoicing occurs in the order lifecycle.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, progressing through a lifecycle from placement to shipment."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order, preventing it from being fulfilled and shipped."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document generated for an order that locks in pricing. Described by Finance as irreversible once created."},{"term":"Placement","definition":"The moment an order is created and accepted into the system."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The point at which goods leave the warehouse for delivery to the customer."},{"term":"Invoicing at Placement","definition":"Operations policy of generating an invoice at the exact moment an order is placed, to lock in pricing."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a product/business policy about the cancellation window. A VP could change this policy (e.g., restrict cancellation to 30 minutes after placement).","testable_assertion":"Given an order in any pre-shipment state, a cancellation request succeeds."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled. Invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance declares invoicing irreversible — this is a financial integrity constraint. Allowing cancellation of invoiced orders would create accounting inconsistencies, phantom revenue, and potential audit/compliance violations.","testable_assertion":"Given an order with an associated invoice, a cancellation request is rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy decision about when invoicing occurs. It could be changed (e.g., invoice at shipment, invoice at pick-pack) without corrupting system state.","testable_assertion":"Given a newly placed order, an invoice is generated synchronously as part of the placement process."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Order placed and immediately cancelled","expected_behavior":"CONTRADICTED: BR-1 says this should succeed (pre-shipment). BR-2 + BR-3 say it must fail (invoiced at placement). No correct behavior can be defined."},{"scenario":"Order in 'processing' or 'picking' state","expected_behavior":"CONTRADICTED: Same fundamental conflict. Order is pre-shipment (BR-1 allows cancel) but already invoiced (BR-2 forbids cancel)."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment — some items shipped, some not","expected_behavior":"Undefined. Even if the contradiction were resolved, partial shipment cancellation rules are unspecified."},{"scenario":"System failure during invoicing at placement","expected_behavior":"If invoice generation fails at placement, the order may exist without an invoice. BR-1 would allow cancellation, BR-2 would not block it. But this is an error state, not a designed workflow."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"HIGH RISK. If we implement BR-1 and allow pre-shipment cancellations, we violate Finance's irreversible invoicing rule, potentially creating orphaned invoices, revenue recognition errors, and audit failures. If we implement BR-2+BR-3 strictly, no order can ever be cancelled, which increases refund/return costs and harms customer experience.","operational":"Deadlock. Operations cannot process cancellations under any consistent reading of these three rules. Implementing any subset creates friction with the excluded stakeholder.","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders before shipment (industry standard). If cancellation is impossible due to immediate invoicing, customer satisfaction and trust will suffer. Support ticket volume will increase.","risk":"Compliance risk if invoiced orders are cancelled without proper credit note/void procedures. Reputational risk if customers cannot cancel orders at all. Implementation risk if engineering guesses which rule wins — the wrong choice cascades into accounting, fulfillment, and customer-facing flows.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel orders if Finance+Ops rules win. Poor experience, increased support contacts, potential churn."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"Accounting integrity at risk if PM rule wins and invoiced orders are cancelled without a compensating transaction (credit note, void)."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"Must change invoicing timing if PM rule wins, potentially losing the price-lock guarantee they require."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the brunt of whichever rule loses — either explaining why cancellation is impossible, or handling accounting corrections."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Cannot implement a consistent system until the contradiction is resolved. Any implementation chosen will be wrong for at least one stakeholder."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"The three requirements form a logical contradiction. BR-1 permits cancellation pre-shipment. BR-2 forbids cancellation post-invoice. BR-3 ensures every order is invoiced at placement. Therefore: every placed order is invoiced (BR-3), invoiced orders cannot be cancelled (BR-2), so no order can ever be cancelled — directly contradicting BR-1. No acceptance criteria can be defined until stakeholders resolve this. Confidence is low because the spec as written is internally inconsistent.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and not a matter of interpretation","BR-2 (invoice irreversibility) is a financial integrity invariant","The three rules taken together make cancellation impossible"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which rule the business actually intends to take priority","Whether Finance would accept a credit-note/void workflow as distinct from 'cancellation'","Whether Operations would accept deferring invoicing to a later lifecycle stage"],"assumptions":["Invoicing and placement are synchronous as described — there is no gap between order creation and invoice generation","The term 'cancelled' means the same thing to all three stakeholders","No credit note or invoice void mechanism exists that could satisfy Finance while enabling cancellation"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The three requirements are mutually exclusive. (1) PM says orders can be cancelled before shipment. (2) Finance says invoiced orders cannot be cancelled. (3) Ops says orders are invoiced at placement. Combining (2) and (3): no placed order can ever be cancelled. This directly contradicts (1). The system cannot satisfy all three rules simultaneously.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Engineering","Customers"],"options":["Option A: PM wins — allow cancellation pre-shipment, but require Finance to accept a credit note / invoice void workflow as a compensating transaction. Invoicing remains at placement but becomes reversible via credit note (not 'cancellation' of the invoice, but a financial offset).","Option B: Finance wins — orders cannot be cancelled once placed. PM must remove the cancellation feature or restrict it to a micro-window before invoice generation (if any async gap exists).","Option C: Operations adjusts — defer invoicing to a later stage (e.g., at pick/pack or at shipment). This preserves both BR-1 and BR-2 but Operations loses the price-lock-at-placement guarantee.","Option D: Hybrid — invoice at placement for price lock, but introduce a 'credit note' or 'void invoice' concept that is distinct from cancellation. Cancellation triggers a credit note, satisfying Finance's rule that the original invoice is never deleted (irreversible) while still allowing the order to be cancelled."],"recommendation":"Option D (Hybrid). Introduce a credit note mechanism. The invoice remains irreversible (satisfying Finance), but a compensating credit note is issued upon cancellation, allowing the order to be cancelled pre-shipment (satisfying PM). Operations keeps invoicing at placement for price lock. This is standard practice in logistics and accounting — invoice reversal via credit note, not deletion. However, ALL THREE stakeholder groups must explicitly agree before engineering proceeds."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Logical contradiction across three stakeholder requirements makes the spec unimplementable as written.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 (cancel before shipment) is impossible when BR-2 (no cancel after invoice) and BR-3 (invoice at placement) are both enforced."}]}
